title: Handling Whitespace and Newlines with scanf() in C

content.problemStatement: - `scanf()` reads formatted input from the user and automatically skips whitespace (spaces, tabs, newlines) for most formats like `%d`, `%f`, and `%s`.¥n- However, when reading a single character with `%c`, `scanf()` **does not skip whitespace by default**. This can cause it to read leftover newline characters from previous inputs.¥n- To skip whitespace (including newlines) before reading a character, add a space before %c in the `scanf()` format string like this:¥n¥n**Syntax**¥n¥n```c¥nscanf(¥
, &ch);¥n```¥n- `scanf`: Function used to read formatted input from the user.¥n- `¥

`: Format string specifying how to read input.¥n     - (space before %c): Skips any whitespace characters (spaces, tabs, newlines) before reading the character.¥n- `%c`: Reads a single character.¥n- `&ch`: Address-of operator & gives the memory location of variable ch where the input character will be stored.¥n¥n---¥n**Example**¥n¥n```c¥n#include¥n¥nint main() {¥n    int num;¥n    char ch;¥n¥n    scanf(¥
, num, ch);¥n¥n    return 0;¥n}¥n```¥n**Expected Output**¥n¥n```default¥n10¥nA¥nNumber: 10, Character: A¥n```
